notes.anchor: someone we can laugh at always
notes.note: Caelius had apparently promised in an earlier letter that his election as curule aedile would provide Cicero with an endless object of amusement — probably referring to a mutual enemy or rival whom Caelius's new office would put in an awkward position.

notes.anchor: I became that very same man myself (you know whom I mean), and acted out all those young men he is always parading
notes.note: Cicero is quoting or parodying a character from Roman comedy — almost certainly the senex (old man) figure from Caecilius Statius's Plocium, a stock type who comically over-reacts to surprising news. 'Those young men he is always parading' refers to the young men in the stock plot.

notes.anchor: By Pollux, what a feat you've performed, and what a deed you've done
notes.note: A verbatim or near-verbatim quotation from Roman comedy (the metre is senarius). Roman letter-writers regularly cited comic plays from memory to add a jocular register; Cicero frequently does so in intimate correspondence.

notes.anchor: The excessive pleasure of the mind in me
notes.note: Another truncated comic quotation, again in comic metre, which Cicero breaks off for comic effect — the reader is expected to complete it. The half-line functions as a winking acknowledgment that his joy is excessive.

notes.anchor: the magnifier of my standing, the avenger not only of my enemies but of my detractors too
notes.note: Cicero measures his personal esteem partly by how effectively his friends punish his enemies; Caelius as curule aedile had power over public spectacles, markets, and street-order, giving him real coercive reach in Roman public life.
